reasoning: The hardcoded credential file fallback is confirmed at lines 975-979 of class-instant-indexing.php. When get_setting('json_key') is called and the file 'rank-math-835b6feb842b.json' exists in plugin_dir_path(__FILE__), the function returns its contents via file_get_contents(), bypassing the database-stored key. The call chain is: send_to_api() at line 416 calls get_setting('json_key') unconditionally for all non-Bing Google API requests. send_to_api() is reached via (a) ajax_rm_giapi() which is protected by check_ajax_referer('giapi-console') + current_user_can('manage_options') — authenticated admin-only AJAX; and (b) publish_post() hooked on save_post_{post_type} — runs server-side with no attacker-controlled input. No attacker-controlled input reaches the $setting parameter of get_setting() at any call site — all callers pass hardcoded literal strings. The finding's vuln_class (hardcoded_credential_path) and detection pattern are accurate: a specific service account JSON filename is baked into production source, constituting a development artifact left in production code. However, reachability of the credential OVERRIDE requires the JSON file to actually be present in the plugin directory — it is not distributed with the plugin, so most deployments are unaffected. The filename itself ('rank-math-835b6feb842b.json') is exposed in plugin source to anyone who can read plugin files. Severity downgraded from high to medium: (1) no unauthenticated path to trigger the code; (2) credential theft only possible if the file exists on the server (not a normal deployment state); (3) the exposure is the filename in source + silent override behavior, not a directly leaked credential. The finding is real and the code pattern matches exactly, but the high severity overclaims given the preconditions required.
